items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m having these nagging doubts again…
Shpak’s got a tape recorder,
and the ambassador’s got a medallion.
items.reference: I’m having these nagging doubts again… Shpak’s got a tape recorder, and the ambassador’s got a medallion.
items.analysis: Matches the approved translation essentially verbatim and preserves the catchphrase rhythm and mock-solemn redundancy (“nagging doubts again…”). The concrete objects are kept literal, enabling the comic comparison. No over-explaining. (Note: some analyses argue the Russian implies ‘had/lost’ rather than ‘has got’, but the provided approved case explicitly endorses this exact rendering, so it is treated as perfect here.)

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Your Majesty—pleased to meet you. Your Majesty—pleased to meet you—Your Majesty. Pleased to meet you—hello, Your Majesty.
items.reference: Hello, Your Majesty—pleased to meet you. Your Majesty—pleased to meet you—Your Majesty. Pleased to meet you—hello, Your Majesty.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the flustered politeness loop, repetition, and wobbling word order; “Your Majesty” is a natural English address and preserves the gag.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; preserves the imperious theatrical tone and quotability.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I'm in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman—I'm in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; keeps the archaic melodrama and the insulting sting of “old woman,” which is crucial for the comedic reaction.

items.translation:
Bricked up!
Bricked up, the demons!
items.reference: Bricked up… bricked up, the demons!
items.analysis: Very close to the approved translation and preserves the masonry image and literal “demons.” The only difference is punctuation/line breaks: using exclamation points instead of ellipses slightly shifts from lamenting realization to sharper outcry, but the catchphrase remains intact and funny.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving Cross can do!
items.reference: That’s what the life-giving Cross can do!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation; keeps the Orthodox formula (“life-giving Cross”) and triumphant explanatory tone.
Identical to the approved translation; short imperative correctly renders the speech act (an order), preserving punchiness/quotability.
Identical to the approved translation; idiomatic entrance quip with the right self-satisfied tone.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar…
Yes! Imported caviar…
Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar… Yes! Imported caviar… eggplant!
items.analysis: Essentially identical to the approved translation and lands the punchline on the final word (“Eggplant!”) while keeping the repeated “caviar” cadence. Works well as a quotable list-to-twist gag.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
through backbreaking labor,
is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,
three Soviet cigarette cases,
a jacket… suede… three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three Soviet cigarette cases, a jacket… suede… three.
items.analysis: Preserves the counting cadence and the trailing “three,” which is the core comic mechanism. “Отечественных” rendered as “Soviet” matches the approved decision for clarity. The surrounding lead-in (“Everything… backbreaking labor… is gone”) is faithful to the broader scene and does not harm the meme unit.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
They’ll cure you!
And you too—they’ll cure you!
And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: They’ll cure you… and you too… and they’ll cure me…
items.analysis: Lexically matches the approved solution (“cure” rather than “heal”), but the delivery shifts: the reference emphasizes ellipses/refrain-like, sing-song repetition; the actual subtitles use exclamation points and add “And you too—” which makes it more emphatic and less deadpan/refrain-like. The joke still lands, but the quotable, hypnotic refrain quality is weakened.

summary.interpretation: 0.18 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.04 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
